claim: 14% of US dog-owning households lost a dog in a 5-year period (2005-2010), not the widely-cited 1-in-3.
stance_target: claim that 1 in 3 pets go missing in their lifetime
scope_conditions: US, 2010, random-digit-dial telephone survey, n=1,015 pet-owning households, 5-year recall period
quoted_support: 14 percent for dogs and 15 percent for cats; and Cat guardians were less likely to find their cat – only 74 percent of lost cats were recovered, while 93 percent of lost dogs were recovered.

claim: 49% of lost dogs in the US are found by neighborhood search, making it the dominant recovery method — not shelter visits.
stance_target: neighborhood proximity search as primary recovery strategy for lost dogs
scope_conditions: US, 2010, Weiss et al. 2012, n=1,015 pet-owning households
quoted_support: Forty-nine percent of dog guardians found their dog by searching the neighborhood, and 15 percent of the dogs were recovered because they were wearing an ID tag or had a micro-chip.

claim: Only 6% of lost dogs and 2% of lost cats in the US are found at a shelter, despite shelters being the default recommendation.
stance_target: shelter-first search strategy as sufficient for recovering lost pets
quoted_support: Only 6 percent of dog guardians and 2 percent of cat guardians found their lost pets at a shelter.

claim: Overall 85% of lost dogs and cats (combined) are recovered when owners actively search.
stance_target: high recovery probability justifying intensive search effort
quoted_support: 85 percent of those lost dogs and cats were recovered
